title: The Fire
weakest_section: 19:59-21:30 range with romantic subplot lacking comedy density

jokes.text: Don't you hate these people that talk into your mouth like you're a clown at a drive-through?
jokes.setup: Jerry discussing conversational distance problems
jokes.punchline: Comparing close talkers to speaking to a drive-through clown
jokes.explanation: Classic Jerry observational humor with a vivid, unexpected comparison. The drive-through clown image is both specific and absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Some people stand too close when they talk to you. These are close talkers. A close talker is usually somebody who physically cannot stand that far away from you while speaking. It's a problem. They're in your space. You try to back up, but they just keep coming. It's like they don't understand the concept of personal space.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's like talking to a clown at a drive-through window. You know, those big clown heads? You pull up to the window, and the clown's right there in your face. You just want your burger and fries, but instead you got this enormous clown head six inches from your nose, taking your order. That's what it's like talking to a close talker.

jokes.text: You know how many people-? Too strong, too weak. Sometimes they give you the three-quarter handshake, just the fingers.
jokes.setup: Discussing handshake problems
jokes.punchline: The specificity of 'three-quarter handshake, just the fingers'
jokes.explanation: Solid observational material with specific terminology that captures a universal experience.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: The specificity of 'three-quarter handshake, just the fingers'

jokes.text: I have actually said to people, 'Hey, the handshake is over.'
jokes.setup: Building on handshake problems with late release
jokes.punchline: Jerry's direct confrontation about ending handshakes
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry character moment - his willingness to make social situations more awkward to address awkwardness. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm done with handshakes. They're outdated, they spread germs, and frankly, I don't know where your hand has been.

jokes.text: That's three strikes. You're out.
jokes.setup: Strong grip, late release, pull-in for close conversation
jokes.punchline: Baseball metaphor for handshake violations
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to baseball rules for social interactions, clean structure.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, a handshake is like a baseball pitch. You need a strong grip, but you can't hold on too long. That's a late release. And if you pull in for a close conversation right after, that's a balk. It's a violation.

jokes.text: I'm getting a little backed up.
jokes.setup: Jerry's been separated from Rachel for three weeks
jokes.punchline: Euphemistic description of sexual frustration
jokes.explanation: Typical Jerry euphemism for sexual matters, character-appropriate but not particularly original.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Three weeks. Three weeks! You know what that is? That's like dog years for a single guy. In dog years, I've aged 21 weeks.

jokes.text: Maybe this will become, like, a cool thing, living with your parents. Then maybe baldness will catch on.
jokes.setup: Jerry living with parents might become cool
jokes.punchline: George's self-deprecating extension to his baldness
jokes.explanation: Perfect George character moment - turning any situation into self-pity about his baldness. Well-structured escalation.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, if Jerry lived with his parents, he'd probably become cool. I lived with my parents my whole life and I'm bald.

jokes.text: Hey, believe me, baldness will catch on. When the aliens come, who do you think they're gonna relate to? Who do you think's gonna be getting tour of the ship? The baldies.
jokes.setup: George defending baldness becoming trendy
jokes.punchline: Aliens will prefer bald people and give them spaceship tours
jokes.explanation: Brilliant George absurdist reasoning - taking his baldness insecurity to cosmic proportions. The specificity of 'tour of the ship' and 'the baldies' makes it memorable.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: And then the aliens come, and they see us, and they think we're the dominant species because we're bald. So they give us spaceship tours.

jokes.text: Did you see the mug on that kid? Wouldn't you like to pass the ketchup to someone like me? Please?
jokes.setup: George explaining why he couldn't refuse Big Brother request
jokes.punchline: Mocking the kid's pathetic appeal with ketchup example
jokes.explanation: Good George cruelty masked as explanation. The ketchup example is specific and captures the pathetic nature well.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: How am I gonna say no to a kid? He comes up to me with those big eyes, 'Will you be my Big Brother?' What am I gonna do, kick him in the shins?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So I'm stuck. I'm his Big Brother now. You know what that means? I gotta take him places, I gotta talk to him, I gotta... I gotta care.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The kid asked me if I liked ketchup. I said, 'Yeah, I like ketchup.' Now he thinks we have a connection. We bond over condiments!

jokes.text: Have you noticed they moved where they do the interview on Jeopardy? Yeah. It was in the middle of Single Jeopardy. Now it's after Single Jeopardy. Yeah. It's much better, isn't it? Oh, no comparison.
jokes.setup: Casual conversation about TV show format
jokes.punchline: The complete seriousness about a minor TV scheduling change
jokes.explanation: Perfect Seinfeld 'about nothing' conversation - treating trivial matters with complete seriousness. Classic show rhythm.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what I hate about TV schedules? They move your show and nobody tells you.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, last week I missed my show because they moved it to a different time slot.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's like they don't want you to watch. They make it impossible.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I had to call the network. I said, 'Where's my show?' They said, 'It's on at nine.' I said, 'It was on at eight!'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's the thing — they expect you to just know. Like it's printed in invisible ink.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm thinking about switching to cable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Cable? George, this is serious.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I know it's serious! That's why I'm considering it!

jokes.text: So I tell Alec that I have to go to Paris for an undetermined amount of time. Then all I have to do is buy postcards and have your parents mail them from Paris.
jokes.setup: George needs to get out of Big Brother commitment
jokes.punchline: Elaborate fake Paris scheme using Jerry's parents
jokes.explanation: Classic George elaborate scheme to avoid responsibility. The specificity and selfishness is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I can't do Big Brother. I'm going to Paris.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Paris? What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm moving to Paris. I got a job there teaching American history.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You're moving to Paris?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yes. I leave next week. It's all arranged. I'm staying with your parents until I find an apartment.

jokes.text: Because George is a deeply disturbed individual.
jokes.setup: Someone asking why George is doing the Paris scheme
jokes.punchline: Jerry's matter-of-fact psychological assessment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry deadpan delivery of harsh truth about George. Economical and character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: He's not happy. George is not a happy man. He's a bald man going into the summer. He's got a very significant bald spot, it's gonna get worse. He's probably a little concerned about how he looks. He wants to try something.

jokes.text: Yeah, or if ever on Fifth Avenue here in New York City. You can get some there.
jokes.setup: Helen mentions Saks Fifth Avenue in Miami
jokes.punchline: Morty's obvious correction about Saks existing in NYC
jokes.explanation: Typical Morty pedantic correction. Character-appropriate but not particularly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: Saks Fifth Avenue in Miami

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: Saks Fifth Avenue? They have Saks Fifth Avenue in New York!

jokes.text: See, when people like you die, the widows, they bring in their wardrobes. They make a bundle.
jokes.setup: Discussing vintage clothing store
jokes.punchline: Kramer's casual discussion of Morty's future death for profit
jokes.explanation: Classic Kramer inappropriate directness about death. The casual business-like discussion of mortality is darkly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm telling you, when your father passes, that's gonna be a nice little score for you.

jokes.text: What happens if the husband dies after the wife? Who brings the clothing in then? I suppose the children do. Yes, I suppose they do.
jokes.setup: Morty asking about clothing donation logistics
jokes.punchline: The morbid conversation becoming uncomfortably specific
jokes.explanation: Extension of the morbid clothing conversation with awkward realization of what they're discussing.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: So when I'm gone, what happens to all my clothes?

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: We'll donate them.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: To who? I mean, who's gonna wear a dead man's pants?

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: The Salvation Army takes them.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: So some guy walks around in my pants, he doesn't know I'm dead?

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: That's right.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: That's very strange. I mean, he's out there, and he's wearing dead man's pants. How would he feel if he knew?

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: He's not going to know, Morty.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: Well, what if he figures it out? What if he's at work, and he thinks, 'Hey, these pants feel like a dead guy's pants!'

jokes.text: What plans? We have plans. Where did you get plans? We have plans.
jokes.setup: Jerry asking about dinner plans
jokes.punchline: Frank's increasingly defensive repetition without explanation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Frank Costanza stubbornness - refusing to explain while getting more agitated. The repetition builds comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what are we doing for dinner?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: We're having dinner!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I know, but what kind of dinner?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: What do you mean what kind? It's dinner! You eat it!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Frank, I'm just asking what's on the menu.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Food! We're eating food! What's the big deal?!

jokes.text: I didn't think you could detect abnormal behaviour among your own kind.
jokes.setup: Jerry's parents criticizing the Costanzas
jokes.punchline: Jerry treating his parents like a different species
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Jerry insult disguised as anthropological observation. 'Your own kind' is perfectly dismissive.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: My parents are always criticizing the Costanzas. Like they're a different species or something. I mean, I treat them like they're from another planet.

jokes.text: A bit of a close talker. A what? You'll see.
jokes.setup: Jerry describing Aaron to his mother
jokes.punchline: Setting up the close talker callback to opening monologue
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup for visual payoff and callback to opening. The 'You'll see' promises comedic revelation.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: He's a close talker, Ma. When he talks to you, he stands about... this close.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: How close?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: He's like right in your face. You got his breath, his spit, everything.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: That's uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Uncomfortable? I had to take a shower after he left.

jokes.text: They're exhausting. It's like being in an asylum.
jokes.setup: Helen explaining why they don't like the Costanzas
jokes.punchline: Comparing them to mental patients
jokes.explanation: Helen's surprisingly harsh assessment. The asylum comparison is vivid and captures the Costanza chaos.

dialogue.speaker: Helen Costanza
dialogue.line: They're like mental patients. They should be in an institution.

jokes.text: Sorry, pal. Wish I could help you out.
jokes.setup: Jerry calling Rachel, getting machine, pleading
jokes.punchline: Jerry's resigned acceptance to the answering machine
jokes.explanation: Jerry's defeated response to technology failing him. The politeness to the machine is mildly amusing.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Hi Rachel, it's Jerry. Listen, I know you're probably there, you're just screening your calls. Pick up! Come on, just pick up for a second. I just want to talk to you for one minute. Please? Okay, well, I'm not going to beg. Actually, I am going to beg. Please pick up the phone. Hello? Are you there? Okay, fine. I guess you're not there. Or you are there and you're not picking up. Either way, I'm talking to a machine. So I'll just say what I have to say to this machine, and hopefully you'll get the message. Literally.

jokes.text: Again with the pepper? What do you gotta use all that pepper for? Keep quiet. You trying to set my mouth on fire?
jokes.setup: Costanzas preparing dinner
jokes.punchline: Typical Costanza bickering over seasoning
jokes.explanation: Standard Costanza domestic argument. Character-perfect but predictable material.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Where's the salt? I need more salt!

dialogue.speaker: Estelle
dialogue.line: Frank, you're gonna give yourself a heart attack with all that salt.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Don't tell me how to eat! I've been eating salt for sixty years.

dialogue.speaker: Estelle
dialogue.line: And look at you! You're all bloated!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Bloated?! This is muscle, Estelle!

jokes.text: Don't you think he had to be nearsighted? Nobody would paint like that if they could see. It's all out of focus. He's from the Impressionist school, you know, like Monet, Manet, Tippy-tippy-day-day.
jokes.setup: Morty discussing Impressionist paintings
jokes.punchline: His practical explanation and made-up artist name
jokes.explanation: Perfect Morty logic - reducing high art to practical vision problems. 'Tippy-tippy-day-day' is brilliantly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: You know, these Impressionist paintings, they're not really pictures of things. They're more like... suggestions of things. The artist just kind of throws paint at the canvas and hopes something recognizable emerges.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: I was looking at one the other day by this French guy, Monet... or was it Manet? No, no, it was definitely Monet Garibaldi. Anyway, it was supposed to be a water lily, but it looked like someone sneezed on a canvas.

jokes.text: Hey, you must be Aaron. I've heard about you.
jokes.setup: Aaron says same line to Kramer
jokes.punchline: Kramer's identical response showing the awkwardness
jokes.explanation: Nice awkward beat where both characters use the same greeting, highlighting the social discomfort.

jokes.text: The Executive was a classic. These haven't been made in 20 years. Why would they? Nobody bought them then.
jokes.setup: Morty proud of his raincoat invention
jokes.punchline: Admitting it was a commercial failure while calling it classic
jokes.explanation: Perfect Morty contradiction - calling something classic that failed commercially. Great self-aware deflation.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: It was a bona fide commercial failure, but it's a classic.

jokes.text: This is like the meeting of Smith and Wesson.
jokes.setup: Morty and Kramer partnering on raincoats
jokes.punchline: Comparing them to famous gun manufacturers
jokes.explanation: Good comparison suggesting their partnership will be destructive. Economical and apt.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: These raincoats, they're like the Colt 45 and the Smith and Wesson of outerwear.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You know, they're precision instruments. Finely crafted. Built to last.

jokes.text: An apartment complex. The Eiffel Towers.
jokes.setup: Asked where he'll stay in Paris
jokes.punchline: Obvious fake name for apartment building
jokes.explanation: George's terrible improvised lie. The obviousness makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'll stay at the Hotel Georgeau.

jokes.text: George, you have no idea how fantastic this is. We've been trying to reunite Joey with his father who lives in Paris. But he's afraid to fly alone.
jokes.setup: George's fake Paris trip
jokes.punchline: His lie perfectly solves a real problem he didn't know about
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic reversal - George's selfish lie creates an unexpected obligation. Classic Seinfeld consequence structure.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm going to Paris.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: Paris? When?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Next month. I need to get away, clear my head.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: But we have the dinner with my parents next month.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I know, but this is important.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, you're not really going to Paris.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? Of course I am.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, you're not. You hate flying.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, it's a good lie.

dialogue.speaker: Susan
dialogue.line: Wait, you're lying to me?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yes, but now you have an excuse to tell your parents why you can't make the dinner.

jokes.text: Gee, what a coincidence.
jokes.setup: Learning his lie created a real obligation
jokes.punchline: George's weak attempt to seem surprised
jokes.explanation: George's obvious discomfort at being trapped by his own lie. The understatement reveals his panic.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What? No, I... I didn't know that.

jokes.text: You didn't feel uncomfortable spending the whole day at the museum with two complete strangers who are more than twice your age? No. It was fun. You had fun with Mr. and Mrs. Seinfeld? Yeah. They bought me a Coke.
jokes.setup: Elaine questioning Aaron's museum trip with Jerry's parents
jokes.punchline: Aaron's simple explanation: they bought him a Coke
jokes.explanation: The childlike simplicity of being won over by a Coke purchase is both innocent and absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So you went to the museum with Jerry's parents?

dialogue.speaker: Aaron
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: And what did you do there?

dialogue.speaker: Aaron
dialogue.line: They bought me a Coke.

jokes.text: So I took out my belt, just to threaten him, and I got a glimpse of myself in the mirror.
jokes.setup: How he invented the beltless coat
jokes.punchline: Threatening Jerry with a belt led to fashion inspiration
jokes.explanation: Good backstory connecting child abuse threat to fashion innovation. Darkly absurd origin story.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, I invented the beltless coat. I was chasing your mother around the bedroom with a belt, and I said, 'Why do we need belts?' So I took the belt off, and I never wore one again.

jokes.text: Who died? Nobody died. Jack, I want you to do me a big favour.
jokes.setup: Morty calling Jack in Florida
jokes.punchline: Jack's assumption that only death warrants a call
jokes.explanation: Good elderly character humor - the morbid practicality of assuming death news.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: Jack? It's Morty. I'm calling you.

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: Morty? What happened? Who died?

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: Nobody died. I just wanted to talk to you.

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: So nobody died and you're calling me?

jokes.text: You think you're gonna sell those old crappy raincoats? That's garbage. I guarantee you, Doris won't let him mail those boxes.
jokes.setup: Jack hearing about the raincoat scheme
jokes.punchline: His dismissive assessment and wife's controlling nature
jokes.explanation: Jack's brutal honesty about the business venture and domestic dynamics.

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: You're using OUR raincoat for this scheme?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: It's a good raincoat!

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: It's a GREAT raincoat.

dialogue.speaker: Estelle
dialogue.line: Jack, don't encourage him!

jokes.text: Maybe it was a mouse. Okay, that's it! We're moving! What? I will not tolerate infestation.
jokes.setup: George suggesting attic noise was a mouse
jokes.punchline: Frank's immediate extreme reaction to move
jokes.explanation: Perfect Frank overreaction - going from maybe a mouse to moving house. The dramatic escalation is character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's probably just a mouse in the wall or something.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: A mouse?! We're moving! I'm not living in a house with mice! Pack your things, we're out of here!

jokes.text: Could you make it 225? That was his high game in bowling.
jokes.setup: Rudy offering $200 for father's clothes
jokes.punchline: George's sentimental negotiation based on bowling score
jokes.explanation: Touching yet absurd negotiation tactic. The specificity of the bowling score makes it both sad and funny.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Two hundred dollars? For my father's clothes? That's not enough. My father was a very important man. He bowled a 201 once.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's right. I'll take three hundred.

jokes.text: They had plans, huh? They had plans! Boy, I've never seen these before. They just came in. Part of my springtime cruise collection.
jokes.setup: Learning Seinfelds had dinner with Kramer
jokes.punchline: George's mounting anger while pretending to shop
jokes.explanation: George's passive-aggressive discovery of the snub. The repetition shows his growing outrage.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. You had dinner? You and Kramer had dinner together?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What, what did you talk about? What could you possibly have talked about?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't understand this. I don't understand how this happened.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know what, I'm gonna shop. I'm gonna look at some... shirts here. Very nice shirts.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: These are quality. Look at the stitching on this. That's craftsmanship.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So what did Kramer say? What did he say about me?

jokes.text: It's gonna be very interesting, very interesting if they don't show up tonight. You know, my mother made all this paella. What is that anyway? It's a Spanish dish. It's a mélange of fish and meat with rice. Very tasty.
jokes.setup: George threatening consequences if Seinfelds don't come
jokes.punchline: Jerry's calm culinary description amid the threat
jokes.explanation: Jerry's complete indifference to George's threats, casually explaining paella like a cooking show.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You better show up, or there's gonna be hell to pay!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What are you making?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm serious, Jerry! This is important!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, I know, I know. But what's the dish?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It doesn't matter what the dish is!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's gonna be a nice marinade, isn't it?

jokes.text: If you think it's too much, they don't have to mail the postcards.
jokes.setup: Jerry objecting to George's Paris scheme escalation
jokes.punchline: George's manipulative negotiation withdrawal
jokes.explanation: Perfect George manipulation - threatening to withdraw the original favor to get the bigger one.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You can't just go to Paris!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Why not? People go to Paris all the time.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Not like this. Not on a whim.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Fine. Forget it. I'm not going.

jokes.text: I was able to finagle two more tickets to My Fair Lady and I thought, why not ask Morty and Helen?
jokes.setup: Aaron arriving for date with Elaine
jokes.punchline: Inviting Jerry's parents on their date
jokes.explanation: Aaron's oblivious romantic sabotage. The enthusiasm for ruining his own date is cringe-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Aaron
dialogue.line: Hey Elaine, I was thinking we could go to that new restaurant tonight.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That sounds great!

dialogue.speaker: Aaron
dialogue.line: Yeah, and I invited Jerry's parents to come along. They're really funny people.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You what?

jokes.text: We better not. They're gonna be here any second. When are they leaving? In two days. It's been so long. I know. Okay. It's only two more days. Right. Thursday, 3:00.
jokes.setup: Jerry and Rachel trying to be intimate
jokes.punchline: Their clinical scheduling of passion around parents' departure
jokes.explanation: The unsexy logistics of adult romance with parents around. The specific time reference makes it more pathetic.

dialogue.speaker: Rachel
dialogue.line: So your parents are leaving tomorrow at 2?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, their flight is at 4.

dialogue.speaker: Rachel
dialogue.line: So we have from 2 to 4?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, they have to get to the airport, so really it's more like 2 to 3:15.

dialogue.speaker: Rachel
dialogue.line: Okay, so we're scheduling sex around your parents' departure time?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I know, it's not romantic.

jokes.text: We saw Regis Philbin get out of a limousine. He looks better on TV.
jokes.setup: Helen describing their day out
jokes.punchline: Casual celebrity disappointment
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about celebrity disappointment. The casual delivery makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: We saw a celebrity today.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Who?

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: I don't know, but he wasn't very tall.

jokes.text: You know, I still got that pen, the one that writes upside down. Yeah. I should have kept it.
jokes.setup: Jack mentioning the pen
jokes.punchline: Jerry's regret about giving it up
jokes.explanation: Reference to famous space pen episode. More nostalgic than funny for viewers familiar with the callback.

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: You know, I've been using that pen you gave me. It's a great pen.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I wish I never gave it to you.

jokes.text: Get the hell out of here with your knob! What does he want from you? My idiot son could open that garage door. What did I do?
jokes.setup: Jack frustrated with garage door instructions
jokes.punchline: His angry outburst and wife's criticism
jokes.explanation: Good Florida retiree humor. Jack's frustration and Doris's immediate criticism are character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: What is this?! These instructions make no sense! You need a Ph.D. in engineering to open a garage door!

dialogue.speaker: Doris
dialogue.line: Jack, it's just a garage door.

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: Just a garage door?! This is an affront to mankind! A mockery of human intelligence!

dialogue.speaker: Doris
dialogue.line: You're overreacting.

dialogue.speaker: Jack
dialogue.line: I'm not overreacting — you're underreacting!

jokes.text: They were drinking champagne in a buggy! First Kramer, then Elaine? Yeah. It's a slap in the face.
jokes.setup: Learning more people had dinner with the Seinfelds
jokes.punchline: Estelle's growing outrage at the snubs
jokes.explanation: Estelle's mounting indignation at being excluded. The champagne detail adds to the insult.

dialogue.speaker: Estelle
dialogue.line: You had dinner with them? When did you have dinner with them?

dialogue.speaker: Estelle
dialogue.line: We never had dinner with them! How could you have dinner with them?

dialogue.speaker: Estelle
dialogue.line: What is this, some kind of joke? Everyone's having dinner with the Seinfelds but us!

dialogue.speaker: Estelle
dialogue.line: I want to know right now - who else have you had dinner with that we don't know about?

jokes.text: A mouse! I saw a mouse! It's the remote.
jokes.setup: Frank panicking about mouse infestation
jokes.punchline: His terror at seeing the TV remote
jokes.explanation: Frank's hysteria making him mistake common objects for mice. Physical comedy of paranoia.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: There's a mouse in the apartment! A mouse! We gotta get it out of here!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: It could be anywhere. In the walls, in the cabinets, under the couch...

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Wait... what's that? Oh my God! Oh my God!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: It's the remote! The remote is on the floor! The mouse could've gotten to it!

jokes.text: Where the hell are my clothes? I love those clothes. Lousy, moth-ridden crap.
jokes.setup: Frank looking for his vacation clothes
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicting his love for them
jokes.explanation: Frank's rapid emotional reversal from loving to hating his clothes. Classic mood swing.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Where are my vacation clothes? I gotta look casual.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: These are my everyday clothes.

jokes.text: They call it vintage, you know, to take your mind off what it really is, nasty-wear.
jokes.setup: Jerry's standup about used clothing
jokes.punchline: Coining 'nasty-wear' as the real term
jokes.explanation: Great Jerry wordplay and insight. 'Nasty-wear' is a perfect comedic coinage that captures the essence.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what they call used clothing? 'Pre-owned.' That's the term now. Pre-owned. Like it's a luxury item. 'Oh, I'm wearing pre-owned.' No, you're wearing nasty-wear. That's what it is. Nasty-wear. Someone else's nasty-wear. That's the real term.

jokes.text: Men hang on to underwear until each individual underwear molecule is so strained it can barely retain the properties of a solid. It actually becomes underwear vapour.
jokes.setup: Men's relationship with underwear
jokes.punchline: Pseudo-scientific description of underwear disintegration
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Jerry standup escalation. The scientific language applied to underwear decay is absurdist perfection. 'Underwear vapour' is inspired.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I think there's a certain point in a man's life when he stops washing his underwear and starts throwing it away. When the elastic goes, it's gone. That's it. You're done. I don't care if it's new, if the elastic is gone, it goes in the garbage.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Because at a certain point, the elasticity just... leaves. It's like the underwear equivalent of a man's life - everything just starts to deteriorate. The waistband separates from the cotton. The stitching comes undone. It's like a textile breakdown.

jokes.text: What'd you think of the black and white? The black and white? The whole movie was in black and white. Oh, yeah, I didn't even realise.
jokes.setup: Jerry testing if his dad actually saw Schindler's List
jokes.punchline: Morty's obvious lie about not noticing it was black and white
jokes.explanation: Good setup for revealing Morty's lie. The obviousness of not noticing black and white makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Dad, did you actually watch Schindler's List?

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: Of course I watched it!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: The whole thing?

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: Yes, the whole thing. It was great.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Did you notice anything... unusual about it?

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: Unusual? No, no. What do you mean?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It was in black and white.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: So? A lot of movies are in black and white.

jokes.text: You're doing all the legwork. That's right. He's ripping you off. You're right. He's ripping me off. You should be getting more. He's ripping me off. Don't let him take advantage of you.
jokes.setup: George complaining about the raincoat deal
jokes.punchline: Kramer's enthusiastic agreement fueling George's paranoia
jokes.explanation: Good George/Kramer dynamic where Kramer validates George's complaints without thinking.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The raincoat deal is ridiculous. I can't believe I agreed to this.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Are you kidding? This is gold, Jerry! Gold!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: What? Why are you so excited about this?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Because it's brilliant! You're gonna make a fortune!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Wait... why do you think I'm gonna make a fortune? What do you know that I don't know?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Nothing! I just think it's a great idea!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, no, no. You have that look. You know something. Is someone else working on a raincoat deal? Is that it?

jokes.text: All right, let's compromise. Twenty-five percent. Okay, it's a deal.
jokes.setup: Kramer demanding more, Morty offering less
jokes.punchline: They 'compromise' back to the original deal
jokes.explanation: Perfect negotiation satire - all that arguing to end up exactly where they started.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I want a hundred dollars.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: I can give you fifty.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Seventy-five.

dialogue.speaker: Morty
dialogue.line: Sixty.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: We've got a deal. A hundred dollars.

jokes.text: You were making out during Schindler's List? I couldn't help it. We hadn't been alone in a long time. It got the better of me.
jokes.setup: Jerry admitting to inappropriate behavior
jokes.punchline: His weak justification for the timing
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe moment - the inappropriateness of the timing makes Jerry's excuse pathetic and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I couldn't help it, it just happened!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Jerry, you're telling me you did that right there?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, technically it was more of a reflex action.

jokes.text: He was moving on her like the storm troopers into Poland.
jokes.setup: Newman describing Jerry's behavior at Schindler's List
jokes.punchline: Comparing his making out to Nazi invasion
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly inappropriate Newman metaphor connecting Jerry's behavior to the movie's subject matter. Dark, specific, and perfectly character-driven.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: He's making out during Schindler's List. It's like the Nazis invading Poland!

jokes.text: We started up a little during the coming attractions and the next thing we knew the war was over.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining the Schindler's List incident
jokes.punchline: Treating WWII as a time marker for their make-out session
jokes.explanation: Perfectly inappropriate Jerry logic - using the Holocaust as a casual time reference. The economy and audacity make it brilliant.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's not a milestone, it's a movie! It's a World War II movie!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So? We went to see Schindler's List.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You can't make out during Schindler's List!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Because it's Schindler's List! It's about the Holocaust!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So we'll wait. We'll make out after the Holocaust.

jokes.text: You stole my shirt, you son of a bitch! George, your friends up in the attic, stealing my clothes! Give me that back. Give me that. Give me.
jokes.setup: Frank recognizing his shirt on Kramer
jokes.punchline: Physical struggle over the shirt
jokes.explanation: Classic Frank explosion leading to physical comedy. The escalation from recognition to assault is character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: That's my shirt!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: No, it's not.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Yes it is! I know my own shirt!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You're crazy, old man.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Give me my shirt!

jokes.text: You're gonna take this kid to Paris? I get a free trip to Paris. I go in the Big Brothers' Hall of Fame. I mail my own postcards.
jokes.setup: Jerry questioning George's Paris plan
jokes.punchline: George's self-serving justifications
jokes.explanation: Perfect George rationalization - turning child abandonment into personal benefits. Character-driven selfishness.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're just going to move to Paris?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's a fresh start. New country, new me.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, you can't even commit to a coffee shop.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's different. This is Paris. Everyone reinvents themselves in Paris.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Name one person who moved to Paris and actually changed.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well... there's me. I'm going to be sophisticated. I'll wear a beret.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A beret? George, you can't even wear a normal hat without looking ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: See, this is exactly why I need to leave. Nobody believes in me here.

jokes.text: He said I was dead? That's right. Squeezed an extra $25 out of me. That's what my life is worth to him, $25?
jokes.setup: Frank learning George said he was dead
jokes.punchline: Frank's outrage at being worth only $25
jokes.explanation: Perfect Frank outrage - not upset about being declared dead, but about the low value placed on his life.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: What?! Twenty-five dollars?! That's it?! I'm worth twenty-five dollars?!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I worked my whole life! I built a business from nothing! And I'm worth twenty-five dollars?!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: George said I was dead and I'm only worth twenty-five dollars?! I could get more than that at a pawn shop!

jokes.text: They need some water. Why? They'll get dehydrated on the plane! Get the Seinfelds some water! Please!
jokes.setup: Aaron's breakdown at the airport
jokes.punchline: His desperate concern for the Seinfelds' hydration
jokes.explanation: Aaron's complete breakdown manifesting as concern for airplane hydration. The specificity makes the hysteria funnier.

jokes.text: A heavyset fellow. I believe he lives in your building.
jokes.setup: Explaining how he learned about Jerry's behavior
jokes.punchline: Describing Newman without naming him
jokes.explanation: Good Newman reference without naming him. 'Heavyset fellow' is appropriately dismissive.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Goldstein
dialogue.line: He's a mail man. He comes to the building, he's always eating, he's very angry...

jokes.text: Where the hell is your father?
jokes.setup: Estelle arrives looking for Frank
jokes.punchline: Her immediate aggressive demand
jokes.explanation: Typical Estelle entrance - immediately demanding and accusatory. Character-consistent but not particularly creative.

dialogue.speaker: Estelle
dialogue.line: Where's Frank?! I'm not leaving until I find him!

jokes.text: ATMs and urinals. I guess whenever someone's taking valuables out of their pants, you wanna give them as much room as possible.
jokes.setup: Jerry's standup about personal distance
jokes.punchline: Connecting ATMs and urinals through valuables/pants logic
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Jerry callback and escalation. The connection between ATMs and urinals through 'valuables from pants' is inspired observational comedy at its best.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what I've realized? ATMs and urinals are basically the same thing. You stand there, you're focused, you don't want anyone near you, and you're dealing with something very personal in your pants.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: The only difference is at an ATM, you're worried about someone looking over your shoulder to see your PIN. At a urinal, you're worried about someone looking over your shoulder period!

tmdb_overview: George embarrasses himself at a kids' birthday party; Jerry heckles back at a heckler.
insight: Cringe-heavy observational comedy underperforms at 67.8 despite strong character work throughout.
